draft: Sketch the layers first: which single control removes the vulnerability, and which ones only reduce the damage if something slips through? Keep the order straight — parameterize first, then validate, then restrict privileges, then fail safely.

fields.label: You let users sort a report by a column name they choose. Why can't the column name be a ? placeholder, and what do you use instead?
options: Placeholders stand in only for values, never for identifiers like column or table names; validate the chosen column against an allowlist of known-good names and reject anything else
You can parameterize a column name exactly like a value with a ?
Escape the column name and concatenate it — escaping makes identifiers safe
Column names can never come from user input under any circumstances

fields.label: Why does a parameterized query stop injection where escaping the input is unreliable?
options: The query structure is compiled before the input is ever seen, and the value is then bound as pure data that is never parsed as SQL — so no input can become code
It automatically deletes any SQL keywords it finds in the input
It encrypts the user's input before running the query
It runs the query inside a transaction it can roll back

fields.label: Why is a blocklist that strips the word UNION from input a weak defense?
options: Blocklists are trivially bypassed — UNunionION, different casing, and encodings all slip through — so they supplement but never replace parameterizing
Blocklists are perfectly safe and make parameterized queries unnecessary
Stripping UNION also breaks every legitimate query, so it can't be used
The database ignores a blocklist unless least privilege is also set

fields.label: Match each defense to the job it actually does.
fields.help: Only one of these removes the hole; the rest limit the damage of a successful attack.
options: Caps the blast radius of a successful injection
Denies attackers a schema map from leaked error text
The only safe way to handle identifiers like a sort column
